Alright, you're back and it's time to continue on to rule number 7 of straight line prospecting, which is you must always use the correct body language as the prospect response. And let me just tell you that this is perhaps the most important rule of all in terms of building rapport. You see, when I speak about getting into rapport with the prospect as you are gathering intelligence, this is specifically what I'm talking about. Watch what I mean. I ask someone a question, right? So I say, so tell me, you know, how long you've been living in this area for, right? Face a question. And as they respond, now watch my by language. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. You're done. By the way, you lost the person right there because in normal communication, when people who respect each other, who are friends or who care about one another, when someone talks to you, you listen with interest. So I say, so how long you're living in the area for? And they say, oh, I've been living here for about two years now. And as they say, I'm like, uh-huh. Oh, really? Mm-hmm. Ooh. I eat. Ooh. I eat those oo's and ah's and ah's and yups. That is what drives building rapport while you gather intelligence. It's about making the icon, scratching your chin. Uh-huh. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Okay. I say facial expressions. This is so crucial when you're in person and by the way, even when you're on the telephone, of course, they can't see the facial expressions, but they hear the uh-huh. Yup. Got it. Uh-huh. I eat. When you're on the telephone and you ask a question and you're prospect, it's responding if you are not making the appropriate grunts and groans, not like, I mean, seriously, right? If you do this the right way, you are building massive lockstep rapport at the highest level. If you are silent, you are dissipating out of that rapport bank faster than you could possibly imagine. So when you're on the phone, right, it's going to be those uh-huh. Yup. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Ooh. Yup. Got it. Yup. Makes sense. Uh-huh. And along with that, sometimes you'll finish someone's sentence if you are 100% sure where they're going, but it shows that you're an expert in your field. It shows that you care, that you're engaged, that you're interested. All the things that matter when it comes to a person saying, you know what? This salesperson actually cares. They're not just in it to make a buck. They care about me and they seem to really know what they're doing. They're like, they're on it. They get me because when you say, ah-ha, yup, got it, they're saying, okay, it's feedback that someone understands what you're saying. Now when you're in person, this is amplified even further by your actual body language, meaning the facial expression you make, how you nod your head, how you scratch your chin thoughtfully. For instance, one of the rules that's crucial here is that when you are listening to someone who's talking about something logic-based about the problem they have, like the ABCs or problem, you're listening for, ah-ha, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. So you're listening to, ah-ha, leaning back slightly, ah-ha, yup, got it, scratch your chin thoughtfully. And then when they start talking about something emotion-based, you want to lean forward, ah-ha, mm-hmm, lean forward into emotion, back from logic. And nothing is worse though than what? Staying constant. In fact, I'll tell you a story. I did a training for one of the largest vacation companies in the world that do vacation sales, right? And they asked me to come in and help with their sales process. They're already a very successful company, but they wanted to go to the next level. And they had me tape the salespeople and then do these little interventions and see what they were doing right wrong. Well, one of the persons who was a novice salesperson, really struggling, taped them in a face-to-face meeting. And I watched and the CEO sat next to me as I pointed out he watched in horror as the salesperson maintained a perfect right angle of body posture for like 60 minutes straight during a sale. It was almost like, that's really tough to do. It was like, bravo to you. How could the guy even do that? But it was so awkward and the energy exchange, it was like zero. And of course, it was a mock sale, but the person was, there's no way he was closing this person, right? That's one example. Then there was a very successful salesperson, a woman who was a great closer and you watched how she leaned forward, she leaned back, she smiled and she kept moving. This is that exchange of power information that dictates how much rapport you build. So again, you must focus on your body language, those ah-has, those ooze-yups when you're listening to your prospect and it will keep you and build massive rapport. Which is perfect, because I'm going to send out to the next rule, which is that you ask questions, you make mental notes on their responses, but don't resolve their pain while you're qualifying. And this is the ultimate rookie mistake. Here's what happens. Rookie salesperson starts asking questions and let's say they found straight line principles so far, right? And they've gotten into rapport and they're asking good questions, they're in the right order and then they ask a question that reveals where the person's pain lies. They say, you know, what's your biggest headache right now in your business? They use the right tonality too, right? What's really keeping up with that person? Well, you know, it's this accounting software I have right now. It's really, I can't keep track of my payable, my receivables, my payables and I'm behind this. Oh, oh, don't worry, problem solved. My program has been, the guy starts saying, don't worry. He's like Horschach from back when I was getting, oh, oh, oh, oh. And they're like, what the hell? Navis alert, it's always the novice button starts flashing, right? That's not what experts do. Here's the deal. Until you know the whole picture, until you know everything that there is to know, the things that you essentially know below the service, but you know, the initial questions dig out the basics, then you go below the service by exhausting lines of questioning. It's only when you know everything there is to know, you know, where they're pain lies fully, then once you got the full picture, then you have earned the right to say, well, Jim, Jill, based on what you just said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. And by the way, that now takes us to our next step, which is your transition. You see, when you're done asking your questions, right? And remember, you hold that pain in harness. I don't want your resolving pain while you qualify. Why? Here's why. Because when you do that, when you qualify someone and you extract the pain point, right? That actually raises up their pain threshold. There's something to feel, they're talking about where their pain lies, right? It's the perfect place you want a prospect living at the moment when you start offering your solution. I want them to consider my solution while they're feeling a bit of uncomfortability because of their pain. It allows them to really consider it in the right light. If you take a buyer in heat, meaning because now you have someone talking about their pain, so you've essentially raised up the heat a bit, right? They're feeling they want to do something about that. And then you're like, oh, make problem solved. Don't worry. Guess what? I have my solution. Don't worry. Don't worry, buddy. It's all good. What do you do? You actually take a buyer in heat and you turn them into a buyer in power. You go the opposite way. So remember, as we qualify, we're also that other process that's going on simultaneously is not only are we separating out the buyers and eat and buyers in power from the look you lose and mistakes, but we're also doing our best. Not always, we can't always do it 100%, but we're doing our best to try to identify the deep pain points, amplify them, and that turns a buyer in power into a buyer in heat. So then when you make your presentation, right, it's from the perspective on their part of feeling pain. Makes them more anxious to buy. Now it creates urgency. If you jump the gun and you try to resolve it there, you did the opposite. You shot yourself in the foot. It's the worst thing that you can do. And that takes us to our next step, which is the transition. But before I dig into that here, let's first stop. There's a lot of information there. I want you to take a quiz. I want you to go through the exercises here in the back end. They're very important here for your success. And then when you're done, you really got this locked in. I will meet you in the next lesson and we'll finish you up with the last two rules of straight line prospecting. I'll see you there.
